However, there are provisions elsewhere in 
Section 1507 that apply to photovoltaic 
products as well as other types of roof 
coverings. 
3111.3.4  
Access and pathways.  Roof 
access, pathways and spacing requirements shall be 
provided in accordance with Section 1205 of SBC 
801.

❖ This section references Section 605.11 of SBC 
801, which, along with its subsections, requires 
that access and paths be provided so that fire 
fighters can perform the important task of 
manual ventilation by cutting one or more holes 
in a building roof. 
3111.3.5  
Ground-mounted 
photovoltaic 
systems. Ground-mounted photovoltaic systems 
shall be designed and installed in accordance with 
Chapter 16 and SBC 801.

❖ Photovoltaic systems that are mounted on the 
ground must meet the structural design 
provisions of Chapter 16 and the provisions of 
Section 1205.5 in SBC 801. The main focus of 
the fire code provisions is the control of 
vegetation around such installations. 
3111.3.5.1  
Fire 
separation 
distances.  
Ground mounted photovoltaic systems shall be 
subject to the fire separation distance requirements 
determined by the law.

❖ The 
code 
does 
not 
contain 
specific 
requirements for ground-mounted photovoltaic 
systems based on fire separation distance. 
Instead, the code defaults to the law for 
guidance 
based 
on 
local 
conditions, 
regulations and ordinances. Essentially, this 
section provides the authority to the jurisdiction 
to enforce necessary separations. 
SECTION 3112 GREENHOUSES 
3112.1  General. The provisions of this section 
shall apply to greenhouses that are designed and 
used for the cultivation, maintenance, or protection 
of plants.

❖ This section assists designers, other code 
users and building officials in more consistently 
applying the requirements for greenhouses. As 
greenhouse food production and scientific 
research becomes increasingly vital and 
sophisticated, this section promotes more 
consistent enforcement of code text, and 
facilitates the introduction of new technology 
related to greenhouses in the future.

The word “greenhouse” is used throughout the 
code, 
and 
important 
requirements 
for 
greenhouses are found in numerous locations 
in the code. These requirements are often 
formatted as exceptions in sections that are 
otherwise unrelated to greenhouses and could 
be easily overlooked by designers and 
enforcers. The purpose of Section 3112 is to 
locate the most relevant code requirements for 
greenhouses in a single location.

Other SBC 201 sections with greenhouse 
requirements are as follows: 
• 
Chapter 3 Occupancy classifications. 
• 
Section 303.4 : Assembly Group A-3 
• 
Section 309.1 : Mercantile Group M 
• 
Section 312: Use Group U 
• 
Section 506: Group U Allowable area 
• 
Section 1103.2.4 : Accessibility exception for 
Group U 
• 
Table 1604.3: Deflection limits 
• 
Section 
1607.14.2.1 
: 
Ordinary 
roofs, 
awnings and canopies 
• 
Section 1609.2 : Structural design and 
protection of openings. 
• 
Section 2405.3 , Exception 3, screening 
• 
Section 2606.11 : Greenhouses and light 
transmitting plastics. 
• 
Section 2607.4 , Exception 3, area limitation 
and separation 
• 
Section 2609.4 , Exception 3, area limitations 
• 
Appendix C: Group U agricultural buildings 
• 
Appendix D: Section D105, exceptions to 
restrictions in fire district.  
• 
Appendix G: Section G114, Utility and 
miscellaneous Group U.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec1507_1_3d95f878
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ Note that there are also numerous other Group 
U 
requirements 
that 
could 
apply 
to 
greenhouses. 
3112.2  Accessibility. 
Greenhouses 
shall 
be 
accessible in accordance with Chapter 11.

❖ While Section 1103.2.4 generally exempts 
greenhouses from the requirements of Chapter 
11, there are cases where Chapter 11 may still 
be applicable. Greenhouses open to the 
general 
public 
must 
be 
provided 
with 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec2606_0_0dd4ac22
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 31—SPECIAL CONSTRUCTION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
1996

accessibility like any other facility. In addition, 
access to paved work areas is required. See 
the commentary to Section 1103.2.4 . 
3112.3  Structural design. Greenhouses shall 
comply with the structural design requirements for 
greenhouses in Chapter 16.

❖ As noted in the commentary to Section 3112.1 
, there are a number of specific allowances or 
requirements for greenhouses. Generally, such 
structures must comply with Chapter 16 unless 
an exception is provided. See the commentary 
to 
Section 
3112.1 
for 
specific 
section 
references. 
3112.4  Glass and glazing. Glass and glazing used 
in greenhouses shall comply with Section 2405.

❖ Section 2405.3 , Exception 3, as noted in the 
commentary to Section 3112.1 , addresses 
greenhouses. Essentially, it is an exception to 
the use of screening for retention of broken 
glass. See the commentary to Section 2405.3  
for more discussion. 
3112.5  Light-transmitting 
plastics.

Light-
transmitting plastics shall be permitted in lieu of 
plain glass in greenhouses and shall comply with 
Section 2606.

❖ Light-transmitting 
plastic 
is 
common 
in 
greenhouses. Such materials are regulated 
within Section 2606. Section 2606 essentially 
sets the baseline requirements while Sections 
2607, 2608 and 2609 are applicable depending 
on how the greenhouse is constructed. As 
noted in the commentary to Section 3112.1 , 
there are several allowances specific to 
greenhouses 
within 
the 
light-transmitting 
plastic requirements. 
3112.6  Membrane structures.  Greenhouses that 
are membrane structures shall comply with Section 
3102.

❖ In some cases, greenhouses are membrane 
structures. Where such a construction is 
chosen compliance with Section 3102 is 
required. 
3112.6.1  
Plastic film.  Plastic films used in 
greenhouses shall comply with Section 3102.3 .

❖ Section 3102.3  contains the combustibility 
criteria for membrane structures. Generally, if 
combustible the greenhouse would need to 
meet the requirements of NFPA 701. There are 
exceptions for nonpublic greenhouses where 
plastic is less than 9 m above the floor or where 
the plastic is less than 20 mil in thickness. See 
the commentary to Sections 3102.3 and 
3102.3.1 . 
SECTION 3113 RELOCATABLE 
BUILDINGS 
3113.1  General. The provisions of this section 
shall apply to relocatable buildings. Relocatable 
buildings manufactured after the effective date of 
this code shall comply with the applicable 
provisions of this code. 
Exception: This section shall not apply to 
manufactured housing used as dwellings.

❖ This section provides a clear and consistent 
path for enforcement professionals, and for 
compliance by owners of relocatable buildings 
who wish to reuse or repurpose their existing 
buildings. It applies to new relocatable 
buildings, including site built relocatable 
buildings. It does not apply to manufactured 
housing used as dwellings. Relocatable 
buildings are defined in Chapter 2.

Unlike site-built buildings, which are typically 
intended to remain on their original site for the 
life of the building, relocatable modular 
buildings are designed and intended for 
relocation, reuse and/or repurposing. Many 
states have statutes that govern the building 
and relocating of relocatable modular buildings. 
For those that do not have state-mandated 
requirements, 
much 
confusion 
and 
inconsistency exists about the requirements for 
relocatable 
modular 
buildings, 
including 
relocatable modular existing buildings.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec2606_1_190bb8e7
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
The 
Modular 
Building 
Institute 
(MBI) 
(www.modular.org) estimated that there were 
over 600,000 code compliant relocatable 
buildings in use in North America in 2016. While 
it is impossible to determine the exact amount 
owned by the public at large, MBI estimates that 
public school districts across North America 
collectively own and operate about 180,000 
relocatable classrooms with the industry 
owning and leasing an additional 120,000. 
Additionally, the industry owns and leases 
approximately 280,000 relocatable buildings for 
various other business occupancies, including 
construction site offices and temporary sales 
offices. 
3113.1.1  
Compliance. A newly constructed 
relocatable building shall comply with the 
requirements of this code for new construction. An 
existing relocatable building that is undergoing 
alteration, addition, change of occupancy or 
relocation shall comply with Chapter 14 of SBC 
901.

❖ New relocatable buildings must comply with 
this code. For existing relocatable buildings, 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3114_0_90678fed
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 31—SPECIAL CONSTRUCTION 
 
 
SBC 201-CC-2024 
1997 
 
the requirements of SBC 901 apply and this 
code does not apply. 
3113.2  Supplemental information. Supplemental 
information specific to a relocatable building shall 
be submitted to the building official. It shall, as a 
minimum, include the following in addition to the 
information required by Section 105: 
1 
Manufacturer’s name and address. 
2 
Date of manufacture. 
3 
Serial number of module. 
4 
Manufacturer’s design drawings. 
5 
Type of construction in accordance with 
Section 602. 
6 
Design loads including roof live load, roof 
floor live load, wind load and seismic site 
class, use group and design category. 
7 
Additional building planning and structural 
design data. 
8 
Site-built structure or appurtenance attached 
to the relocatable building.

❖ The information required in Sections 3113.2  
and 3113.3 provide information to building 
officials that is useful when determining 
whether a relocated modular building is 
compliant with the code’s requirements in a 
new location or jurisdiction. 
3113.3  Manufacturer’s data plate.  
Each 
relocatable module shall have a data plate that is 
permanently attached on or adjacent to the electrical 
panel, and shall include the following information: 
1 
Occupancy group. 
2 
Manufacturer’s name and address. 
3 
Date of manufacture. 
4 
Serial number of module. 
5 
Design roof live load, design floor live load, 
wind and seismic design. 
6 
Approved quality assurance agency or 
approved inspection agency. 
7 
Codes and standards of construction. 
8 
Envelope thermal resistance values. 
9 
Electrical service size. 
10 Fuel-burning equipment and size. 
11 Special limitations if any.

❖ The information required in Sections 3113.2  
and 3113.3 provide information to building 
officials that is useful when determining 
whether a relocated modular building is 
compliant with the code’s requirements in a 
new location or jurisdiction. 
3113.4  Inspection agencies.  The building official 
is authorized to accept reports of inspections 
conducted by approved inspection agencies during 
off-site construction of the relocatable building, and 
to satisfy the applicable requirements of Sections 
110.3 through 110.3.12.1.

❖ Generally, 
this section 
allows 
approved 
agencies to determine whether relocatable 
buildings 
constructed 
off-site 
meet 
the 
requirements of this section. The inspections 
must comply with requirements of Sections 
110.3 through 110.3.12.1. 
SECTION 3114 PUBLIC USE 
RESTROOM BUILDINGS IN FLOOD 
HAZARD AREAS  
Not applicable to KSA 
SECTION 3115 INTERMODAL 
SHIPPING CONTAINERS  
3115.1  General. The provisions of Section 3115 
and other applicable sections of this code shall 
apply to intermodal shipping containers that are 
repurposed for use as buildings or structures, or as 
a part of buildings or structures. 
Exceptions: 
1 
Intermodal shipping containers previously 
approved as existing relocatable buildings 
complying with Chapter 14 of SBC 901. 
2 
Stationary storage battery arrays located in 
intermodal shipping containers complying 
with Chapter 12 of SBC 801. 
3 
Intermodal shipping containers that are listed 
as equipment complying with the standard 
for equipment, such as air chillers, engine 
generators, modular data centers, and other 
similar equipment. 
4 
Intermodal shipping containers housing or 
supporting experimental equipment are 
exempt from the requirements of Section 
3115, provided that they comply with all of 
the following: 
4.1. Such units shall be single stand-
alone units supported at grade level and 
used only for occupancies as specified 
under Risk Category I in  Table 1604.5. 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3115_0_d0e91a65
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 31—SPECIAL CONSTRUCTION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
1998

4.2. Such units are located a minimum of 
2400 mm from adjacent structures and 
are not connected to a fuel gas system or 
fuel gas utility. 
4.3. In hurricane-prone regions and flood 
hazard areas, such units are designed in 
accordance 
with 
the 
applicable 
provisions of Chapter 16.

❖ The intent of these provisions is to provide 
specific requirements to consistently and safely 
regulate intermodal shipping containers. The 
provisions were added to this code based on 
requests by building officials in the US to 
develop a set of consistent code provisions 
which cover the minimum safety requirements 
but which do not duplicate existing code 
provisions. See Commentary Figure 3115.1(1), 
Figure 3115.1(2) and Figure 3115.1(3) for 
examples of an intermodal shipping container 
intended to be regulated by these provisions.

Prior to these provisions several jurisdictions 
created their own regulations or ordinances, or 
administered additional requirements beyond 
the code, in an effort to ensure a safe structure. 
These provisions cover several topics: 
• 
Exceptions to differentiate the intermodal 
shipping container from other code sections 
that could be interpreted as applying to 
intermodal shipping containers under other 
applications 
(e.g. 
temporary 
storage, 
housing special equipment or experimental 
research, 
relocatable 
buildings, 
energy 
storage facilities and listed equipment). 
• 
Verification of a container's construction, 
condition, and structural integrity to assist the 
structural engineer in the evaluation for 
building construction. 
• 
References to other sections of this code 
concerning 
foundations, 
decay, 
termite 
control, 
crawl 
space 
ventilation, 
roof 
assemblies, 
interior 
finishes 
and 
joints/intersections. 
• 
Introduction of structural provisions that are 
unique to intermodal shipping containers, do 
not 
duplicate 
the 
existing 
structural 
requirements and provide a reference to 
appropriate ISO standards on the topic.

❖ Intermodal shipping containers are defined in 
Chapter 2. These provisions further clarify the 
difference 
between 
intermodal 
shipping 
containers in accordance with Section 3115 as 
compared to other types of portable storage 
containers. 
In 
those 
cases, 
the 
term 
“intermodal shipping containers” is intentionally 
not used. 
3115.2  Construction 
documents. 
The 
construction documents shall contain information 
to verify the dimensions and establish the physical 
properties of the steel components and wood floor 
components of the intermodal shipping container, 
in addition to the information required by Sections 
107 and 1603.

❖ These provisions require that information be 
contained in the construction documents that 
are beyond what is required in the Section 107 
“Submittal Documents” 
and 
Chapter 
16 
“Construction Documents” provisions. 
3115.3  Intermodal 
shipping 
container 
information. Intermodal shipping containers shall 
bear an existing data plate containing the following 
information as required by ISO 6346 and verified 
by an approved agency. A report of the verification 
process and findings shall be provided to the 
building owner. 
1 
Manufacturer's 
name 
or 
identification 
number. 
2 
Date manufactured. 
3 
Safety approval number. 
4 
Identification number. 
5 
Maximum operating gross mass or weight 
(kg). 
6 
Allowable stacking load for 1.8G (kg). 
7 
Transverse racking test force (Newtons). 
8 
Valid maintenance examination date. 
Where approved by the building official, the 
markings and existing data plate are permitted to be 
removed from the intermodal shipping containers 
before they are repurposed for use as buildings or 
structures or as a part of buildings or structures.

❖

❖ These provisions focus on identifying or 
verifying the characteristics of the intermodal 
shipping container prior to it being repurposed. 
In this case the provisions require a straight-
forward inspection by an approved agency, 
and verification of the data plate and the 
information contained therein which is normally 
found on intermodal shipping containers. See 
Commentary Figure 3115.3 for an example 
data plate.

The approved agency is not specified for two 
reasons: 
1 
To allow the building official to handle this 
aspect recognizing that each jurisdiction's 
requirements may be different. 
-

CHAPTER 31—SPECIAL CONSTRUCTION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
1999

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3115_1_2851ae03
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
2 
To avoid indirectly dictating an international 
agreement used by shipping and container 
manufacturers worldwide onto jurisdictions.

❖ In this case, the standards are regulated by the 
International Convention of Safe Containers 
(CSC), which has policies and procedures for 
inspecting 
containers 
worldwide. 
These 
procedures include policies for Approved 
Continuous Examination Program (ACEP) at 
the time the container is used in production, 
and policies for third-party inspection agencies. 
The list shown in this section is an excerpt from 
the ISO standard and serves as a reference of 
items to be verified in order to validate the type 
of container. 
3115.4  Protection against decay and termites. 
Wood structural floors of intermodal shipping 
containers shall be protected from decay and 
termites in accordance with the applicable 
provisions of Section 2304.12.1.1 .

❖ Since intermodal shipping containers are built 
with marine grade plywood flooring, A pointer 
is provided to the provisions of SBC 201 
addressing termite damage. 
3115.5  Under-floor 
ventilation. 
The 
space 
between the bottom of the floor joists and the earth 
under any intermodal shipping container, except 
spaces occupied by basements and cellars, shall be 
provided with ventilation in accordance with 
Section 1202.4 .

❖ Underfloor ventilation is necessary to remove 
moisture and moderate temperatures to 
prevent decay and rot of the intermodal 
shipping container. In some cases, repurposed 
intermodal shipping container buildings may 
have a basement or cellar, which would avoid 
potential moisture issues. The language of this 
section is consistent with Section 1202.4 . See 
the commentary to Section 1202.4 . 
3115.6  Roof assemblies. Intermodal shipping 
container roof assemblies shall comply with the 
applicable requirements of Chapter 15. 
Exception: Single-unit, stand-alone intermodal 
shipping containers not attached to, or stacked 
vertically 
over, 
other 
intermodal 
shipping 
containers, buildings or structures.

❖ This section is merely a pointer to Chapter 15 
for roofing requirements. Unless the intermodal 
shipping container is a single, stand-alone unit, 
the roofing requirements of the intermodal 
shipping container buildings are required to be 
consistent with all other building types. 
3115.7  Joints and voids. Joints and voids that 
create concealed spaces between connected or 
stacked intermodal shipping containers at fire-
resistance-rated walls, floor or floor/ceiling 
assemblies and roofs or roof/ceiling assemblies 
shall be protected by an approved fire-resistant joint 
system in accordance with Section 715.

❖ This provision is provided to address the 
interstitial spaces that may be created where 
multiple intermodal shipping containers are 
connected or stacked, whereby that concealed 
space between the containers is intended to be 
protected to prevent fire and hot gasses from 
passing between containers. 
3115.8  Structural. Intermodal shipping containers 
that conform to ISO 1496-1 and are repurposed for 
use as buildings or structures, or as a part of 
buildings or structures, shall be designed in 
accordance with Chapter 16 and this section.

❖ This 
section 
introduces 
the 
necessary 
structural requirements for shipping containers. 
The structural provisions are divided into 
multiple categories, as follows:

1 
The general characteristics for all containers. 
2 
Engineered structural design. 
3 
Simplified method for single-unit, stand-
alone intermodal shipping containers. 
3115.8.1  
Foundations. Intermodal shipping 
containers repurposed for use as a permanent 
building or structure shall be supported on 
foundations or other supporting structures designed 
and constructed in accordance with Chapter 16 
through 23.

❖ Where used as a permanent building or 
structure, intermodal shipping containers are 
required to be secured onto a foundation that 
must comply with the appropriate provisions in 
Chapters 16 through 23. There are no specific 
requirements for foundations within Section 
3115. 
3115.8.1.1  
Anchorage. Intermodal shipping 
containers shall be anchored to foundations or other 
supporting structures as necessary to provide a 
continuous load path for all applicable design and 
environmental loads in accordance with Chapter 16.

❖ This section outlines the two options for 
securing the repurposed intermodal shipping 
container: (1) By placing an intermodal 
shipping container on a foundation, or (2) By 
integrating the intermodal shipping container 
within another structure. This provision makes 
it clear that the load path and anchorage shall 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3115_2_01a7801f
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 31—SPECIAL CONSTRUCTION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2000

be in accordance with the design and 
environmental loads addressed in Chapter 16. 
3115.8.2  
Welds. 
New 
welds 
and 
connections shall be equal to or greater than the 
original connections.

❖ This section requires that any new welds be 
designed and installed with welds of greater 
structural capabilities. This will maintain or 
provide greater structural stability to the 
repurposed intermodal shipping container. 
3115.8.3  
Structural design. The structural 
design for the intermodal shipping containers 
repurposed for use as a building or structure, or as 
part of a building or structure, shall comply with 
Section 3115.8.4 or 3115.8.5 .

❖ This section is merely introducing the two 
methods available for structural design of 
intermodal shipping containers used as a 
building or structure. The first method is a more 
detailed structural analysis for a structure 
made up of anything more than a single stand-
alone intermodal shipping container and in 
some cases single shipping containers used in 
a way that requires a higher level of design 
review. The second option is a simplified 
structural method that is applicable only to 
stand-alone shipping containers under specific 
conditions. 
3115.8.4  
Detailed design procedure. A 
structural analysis meeting the requirements of this 
section shall be provided to the building official to 
demonstrate the structural adequacy of the 
intermodal shipping containers. 
Exception: 
Intermodal 
shipping 
containers 
designed in accordance with Section 3115.8.5 .

❖ The detailed analysis engineering approach 
represents the general engineering practice 
allowed for all other types of building 
construction. For this section, the engineer of 
record is allowed to practice as they normally 
would for any other building type. Note that 
much of this section requires submission 
through the alternative means and methods 
provisions in order to obtain a permit, as 
information 
about 
intermodal 
shipping 
containers is not readily listed in SBC 201 
provisions or referenced standards. 
3115.8.4.1  
Material properties. Structural 
material properties for existing intermodal shipping 
container steel components shall be established by 
material testing where the steel grade and 
composition 
cannot 
be 
identified 
by 
the 
manufacturer's designation as to manufacture and 
mill test.

❖ In 
order 
to 
understand 
the 
structural 
performance of the container, the material 
properties of the steel must be determined. If 
this information is not provided by the 
manufacturer's designation, the materials must 
be tested in order to ascertain the properties of 
the steel. 
3115.8.4.2  
Seismic design parameters. The 
seismic force-resisting system shall be designed and 
detailed in accordance with one of the following: 
1 
Where all or portions of the corrugated steel 
container sides are considered to be the 
seismic force-resisting system, design and 
detailing shall be in accordance with SBC 
301, Table 12.2-1 requirements for light 
frame bearing-wall systems with shear panels 
of all other materials. 
2 
Where portions of the corrugated steel 
container sides are retained but are not 
considered to be the seismic force-resisting 
system, an independent seismic force-
resisting system shall be selected, designed 
and detailed in accordance with SBC 301, 
Table 12.2-1. 
3 
Where portions of the corrugated steel 
container sides are retained and integrated 
into a seismic force-resisting system other 
than as permitted by Item 1, seismic design 
parameters shall be developed from testing 
and analysis in accordance with Section 
104.11 and SBC 301, Section 12.2.1.1 or 
12.2.1.2.

❖ This section lays out how the seismic force-
resisting system must be evaluated This 
analysis will be based on whether the sides 
(e.g., corrugated steel panels and/or steel tube 
frame) of the container are fully retained and 
are used as part of the seismic force resisting 
system or only some sides are retained.

Where some sides are retained, the analysis 
must be either based on providing an 
independent structural system to create the 
seismic force-resisting system or the remaining 
container sides constituting part of the system.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3115_3_1ab85d6f
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
Each scenario requires its own design path. 
3115.8.4.3  
Allowable shear value. 
The allowable shear values for the intermodal 
shipping container corrugated steel sheet panel side 
walls and end walls shall be demonstrated by testing 
and analysis in accordance with Section 104.11. 
Where penetrations are made in the side walls or 
end walls designated as part of the lateral force-
-

CHAPTER 31—SPECIAL CONSTRUCTION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2001

resisting system, the penetrations shall be 
substantiated by rational analysis.

❖ The evaluation of shear values for shipping 
containers is fully a case-by-case situation 
when using the detailed design method. There 
a number of different configurations that can be 
created with intermodal shipping containers 
and each situation needs to be treated 
individually to fully assess the shear values for 
the overall structure. Penetrations in the side or 
end walls can decrease the shear values. Such 
penetrations must be specifically analyzed to 
see what impact those openings have on the 
overall shear values. 
3115.8.5  
Simplified structural design of 
single-unit containers. Single-unit intermodal 
shipping containers conforming to the limitations of 
Section 3115.8.5.1 shall be permitted to be designed 
in accordance with the simplified structural design 
provisions of Section 3115.8.5.2 .

❖ The concept for the single-container approach 
is to make the design and construction process 
simpler for single-unit applications. Example 
uses might include ticket offices located at a 
stadium parking lot, drive through retail or other 
services or a guard station.

The provisions include a strict listing of 
limitations for use of these provisions. The 
requirements 
provide 
structural 
design 
information and pre-established shear wall 
information that is contained in the ISO 1496-1 
standard, which is used to design and construct 
intermodal shipping containers. The shear wall 
values were derived from ISO 1496-1 through 
engineering analysis using a factor of safety of 
5. In addition, there are requirements limiting 
the number and size of openings and service 
holes within the container, as well as to prevent 
building owners or designers from embellishing 
the size to something most engineers would 
define as an opening. This method is intended 
to address the simple structure approach and 
provide available information for use by the 
structural engineer to supplement their work. 
3115.8.5.1  
Limitations. The use of Section 
3115.8.5 is subject to the following limitations: 
1 
The intermodal shipping container shall be a 
single-unit, stand-alone unit supported on a 
foundation and shall not be in contact with or 
supporting any other shipping container or 
other structure. 
2 
The intermodal shipping container top and 
bottom rails, corner castings, and columns or 
any portion thereof shall not be notched, cut, 
or removed in any manner. 
3 
The intermodal shipping container shall be 
erected in a level and horizontal position with 
the floor located at the bottom. 
4 
The intermodal shipping container shall be 
located in Seismic Design Category A, B, C 
or D.

❖ This section scopes the applicability of the 
simplified structural design method for stand-
alone single intermodal shipping container 
structures. The intermodal shipping container 
can have no connections to other intermodal 
shipping containers or structures. Additionally, 
single shipping containers must be located on 
a foundation, retain the structural integrity of 
the container by leaving many aspects such as 
bottom rails and columns as manufactured, 
and be in an upright position as it was designed 
to be used. This method cannot be used within 
Seismic Design Category F. If these limitations 
cannot be met, then the detail design analysis 
as specified in Section 3115.8.4 must be used. 
3115.8.5.2  
Simplified 
structural 
design. 
Where permitted by Section 3115.8.5.1 , single-
unit, stand-alone intermodal shipping containers 
shall be designed using the following assumptions 
for the corrugated steel shear walls: 
1 
The appropriate detailing requirements 
contained in Chapters 16 through 23. 
2 
Response modification coefficient, R = 2. 
3 
Overstrength factor, Ø< = 2.5. 
4 
Deflection amplification factor, [ = 2. 
5 
Limits on structural height, ℎ] = 2900 mm.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3115_4_ee461610
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ This simply sets out the structural design 
criteria for single-unit containers that fall within 
the scope of Section 3115.8.5 . Basically, this 
is a prescriptive design approach not requiring 
a detailed analysis. 
3115.8.5.3  
Allowable shear. The allowable 
shear for the corrugated steel side 
walls 
(longitudinal) and end walls (transverse) for wind 
design and seismic design using the coefficients of 
Section 3115.8.5.2 shall be in accordance with 
Table 3115.8.5.3, provided that all of the following 
conditions are met: 
1 
The total linear length of all openings in any 
individual side wall or end wall shall be 
limited to not more than 50 percent of the 
length of that side wall or end wall, as shown 
in Figure 3115.8.5.3(1). 
2 
Any full-height wall length, or portion 
thereof, less than 1.2 m shall not be 
-

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3116_0_8302d9bb
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
CHAPTER 31—SPECIAL CONSTRUCTION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2002

considered as a portion of the lateral force-
resisting system, as shown in Figure 
3115.8.5.3(2). 
3 
All side walls or end walls used as part of the 
lateral force-resisting system shall have an 
existing or new boundary element on all sides 
to form a continuous load path, or paths, with 
adequate strength and stiffness to transfer all 
forces from the point of application to the 
final point of resistance, as shown in Figure 
3115.8.5.3(3). 
4 
Where openings are made in container walls, 
floors or roofs, for doors, windows and other 
openings: 
4.1. The openings shall be framed with 
steel elements that are designed in 
accordance with Chapters 16 and 22. 
4.2. The cross section and material grade 
of any new steel element shall be equal to 
or greater than the steel element 
removed. 
5 
A maximum of one penetration not greater 
than 150 mm in diameter for conduits, pipes, 
tubes or vents, or not greater than 10350 mm2 
for electrical boxes, is permitted for each 
individual 2.4 m length of lateral force-
resisting wall. Penetrations located in walls 
that are not part of the lateral force-resisting 
system shall not be limited in size or quantity. 
Existing intermodal shipping container vents 
shall not be considered a penetration, as 
shown in Figure 3115.8.5.3(4). 
6 
End wall doors designated as part of the 
lateral force-resisting system shall be welded 
closed.

❖ This section sets out how the allowable shear 
values are determined. Much of the criteria 
required by this section is addressed also by 
the referenced figures within the code text.

Part of this process limits size of openings, 
which openings affect use in providing shear 
strength such as full height doors, requirements 
for framing of openings and maximum size of 
penetrations for building services and systems 
such as plumbing, ventilation and electrical. 
The values for types of containers based on 
meeting the criteria in Section 3115.8.5.3 are 
found in Table 3115.8.5.3. As you will see, the 
values for shear in the side walls vary as the 
container becomes more compact but the end 
wall value is consistent. The more compact a 
container the higher the ability to resist lateral 
loads.

Figure 3115.8.5.3(1) provides the maximum 
length of openings inside walls or end walls to 
limit the reduction in shear strength. An 
intermodal shipping container 12 m in length 
would be allowed to have 6 m open on that 
individual side.

Figure 3115.8.5.3(2) is focused on how the 
openings can be broken up along the side or 
end of the intermodal shipping container. In 
other words, anything less than 1.2 m would be 
considered as an opening and could not be 
counted as part of the minimum length needed 
for lateral strength. Continuing with the example 
of a shipping container that is 12 m in length, in 
order for the 6 m of unopened side wall to be 
considered 50 percent of the total wall length, 
no section could be less than 1.2 m. For 
example, it could be broken into four 1.5 m 
sections but could not be broken into sections 
of 5.1 m and 0.9 m.

Section 3115.8.5.3 , Item 3 requires that all 
boundary elements form continuous load paths. 
Figure 3115.8.5.3(3) figure sets out where 
these boundary elements are required to be 
located.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3116_1_6bd713b9
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
Item 5 of Section 3115.8.5.3 limits the opening 
for one penetration for every 2.4 m length of 
walls 
providing 
lateral 
strength. 
Figure 
3115.8.5.3(4) shows where these limits are 
applied. It also points out an existing vent that 
is permitted to remain. 
SECTION 3116 TELECOMMUNICATION 
SYSTEM IN BUILDING 
This section covers communication circuits and 
equipment in buildings  
3116.1  Telecommunication circuit  The access 
point   junction and pull-box conduit ducts trays 
pathways 
electromechanical 
elements 
of 
a 
telecommunication circuit are recommended to be 
designed and constructed as per the reference guide 
(CST-GUIDE-101.3-24).

chunks.content:
CHAPTER 32—ENCROACHMENTS INTO THE PUBLIC RIGHT-OF-WAY

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2022

—ENCROACHMENTS INTO THE PUBLIC RIGHT-
OF-WAY

❖ General Comments

Chapter 32 contains provisions that regulate a 
variety 
of 
structure 
encroachments 
and 
projections into the public right-of-way, such as 
a sidewalk, parking lot or street. Section 3202 
addresses encroachments on street lot lines 
below grade, above grade, below 2.4 m in 
height and 2.4 m or more above grade. 
Encroachments and projections have to be 
taken into consideration in addition to means of 
egress, fire resistance, fire protection and 
structural and material considerations.

❖ Purpose

The purpose of Chapter 32 is to regulate 
projections and encroachments of structures 
into the public right-of way. 
SECTION 3201 GENERAL 
3201.1  Scope. The provisions of this chapter shall 
govern the encroachment of structures into the 
public right-of-way.

❖ These provisions apply only to encroachments 
on the public right-of-way, not on interior lot 
lines. The location of the street lot line (public 
right-of-way) is determined through a variety of 
methods based on the regulations of the law 
and, often, the government. The location and 
width of streets are often established when an 
area is platted and the land is subdivided into a 
series of blocks, lots and streets. While 
subdivision and zoning laws may provide for 
standard street and right-of-way width, many 
areas of a jurisdiction may predate those 
standards. Streets and highways are often 
established by the Ministry of Transportation 
and regional transportation branches. Such 
agencies can create new streets or widen 
existing streets by either purchasing or 
condemning land of sufficient width. In other 
circumstances, public streets are not owned by 
a city, but they are easements across privately 
owned lands. Because of the wide variety of 
ways in which a public right-of-way can be 
established, it is important for the building 
official to know how rights-of-way were 
established within the jurisdiction and to be 
able to provide this information to permit 
applicants. 
3201.2  Measurement. The projection of any 
structure or portion thereof shall be the distance 
measured horizontally from the lot line to the 
outermost point of the projection.

❖ This section defines how the extent of 
encroachment is determined. The extent of 
encroachment of any structure or appendage 
is to be measured from the established street 
lot line to the outermost (furthest) point of the 
projection. 
3201.3  Other laws. The provisions of this chapter 
shall not be construed to permit the violation of 
other laws or ordinances regulating the use and 
occupancy of public property.

❖ The projections permitted in this chapter are 
also subject to other limitations identified in 
applicable 
laws 
and 
ordinances. 
Other 
applicable laws, for example, are those 
established by the governing authority in 
regulating right-of-way for highways, streets or 
public areas adjacent to the structure in 
question. The street lot line occurs on each 
side of a structure that faces or fronts on a 
street. Many property descriptions have the 
centerline of the public way as the property line. 
A lot line differs in that it separates a lot from a 
public place such as a street or a park. In these 
cases, the lot line is defined not by the 
centreline of the public way, but by the line that 
identifies where the public way exists. This 
chapter only applies to encroachment across 
lot lines, established between a lot and any 
adjoining public right-of-way (street, alley, 
highway) (see commentary, Section 3201.1 ). 
This chapter does not apply to interior lot 
lines—those separating one lot from another. 
Encroachments across interior lot lines are not 
permitted. 
3201.4  Drainage. Drainage water collected from a 
roof, awning, canopy or marquee, and condensate 
from mechanical equipment shall not flow over a 
public walking surface.

❖ A great number of street projections are 
canopies or mansard roofs. They have the 
potential to create a hazardous condition due 
to improper drainage on the walkways they 
cover. It is very important that all street 
projections have proper drainage systems. 
-

CHAPTER 32—ENCROACHMENTS INTO THE PUBLIC RIGHT-OF-WAY

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2023
chunks.section_title: addresses encroachments on street lot lines

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3202_1_e1d845bb
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
SECTION 3202 ENCROACHMENTS 
3202.1  Encroachments 
below 
grade. 
Encroachments below grade shall comply with 
Sections 3202.1.1 through 3202.1.3 .

❖ Allowable projections of below-grade structural 
components 
are 
described 
in 
Sections 
3202.1.1 through 3202.1.3 . The term “grade” 
as used in this chapter should be considered 
the finished ground level of the site. “Grade” 
should not be confused with “grade plane,” 
which is used for determining other code 
standards such as building height and number 
of stories. 
3202.1.1  
Structural support. A part of a 
building erected below grade that is necessary for 
structural support of the building or structure shall 
not project beyond the lot lines, except that the 
footings of street walls or their supports that are 
located not less than 2.4 m below grade shall not 
project more than 300 mm beyond the street lot line.

❖ One of the permissible street lot line 
encroachments (footings) is defined in this 
section. Where a structure is built immediately 
adjacent to the street lot line, the footing is 
permitted to project across that line (see 
Commentary Figure 3202.1.1). Projection of 
footings across side (interior) lot lines and onto 
adjacent property is not permitted. 
3202.1.2  
Vaults 
and 
other 
enclosed 
spaces. The construction and utilization of vaults 
and other enclosed spaces below grade shall be 
subject to the terms and conditions of the applicable 
governing authority.

❖ Below-grade vaults and other enclosed spaces 
that open at the sidewalk level are usually 
covered with solid structural caps (e.g., 
concrete slabs) and are permitted to project 
beyond the building line or street lot line. 
However, since these structures occur in the 
public right-of-way, they are subject to the law 
governing private use of the public right-of-way 
as well as the approval of the applicable 
governing authority. 
3202.1.3  
Areaways. Areaways shall be 
protected by grates, guards or other approved 
means.

❖ Areaways, which provide natural light or 
ventilation to below-grade spaces, are required 
to be protected by grates, guards or other 
approved 
means 
of 
protection 
(see 
Commentary Figure 3202.1.3). Regulations 
allowing the private use of the public right-of-
way may prescribe how areaways are covered 
and protected. Some 
jurisdictions 
allow 
material lifts to be located in these areaways 
and open to the public street. 
3202.2  Encroachments above grade and below 
2.4 m in height. Encroachments into the public 
right-of-way above grade and below 2.4 m in height 
shall be prohibited except as provided for in 
Sections 3202.2.1 through 3202.2.3 . Doors and 
windows shall not open or project into the public 
right-of-way.

❖ This section prohibits all projections into the 
public right-of-way that are above grade and 
below 2.4 m, except those specifically allowed 
by Sections 3202.1 through 3202.2.3 and 
3202.4 (e.g., steps, architectural features such 
as columns or pilasters, awnings, temporary 
vestibules 
and 
storm 
enclosures). 
The 
maximum 
permitted 
encroachments 
and 
minimum clearances, as well as additional 
protection requirements, for the specified 
allowed components are detailed in their 
respective sections. See the commentary to 
Section 3202.1 regarding the term “grade.” 
3202.2.1  
Steps. Steps shall not project more 
than 300 mm and shall be guarded by approved 
devices not less than 0.9 m in height, or shall be 
located between columns or pilasters.
chunks.section_title: addresses encroachments on street lot lines

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3202_2_297a2d8d
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ Steps are permitted to project a maximum of 
300 mm into the public right-of-way by this 
section. However, since steps may pose a 
tripping hazard, an approved device (guard), 
ornamental column or pilaster must be 
provided at a minimum height of 0.9 m to 
minimize this hazard. Commentary Figure 
3202.2.1 illustrates compliance with this 
provision. Where such steps are part of the 
building’s means of egress system, Sections 
1011.11  and 1014 may require the placement 
of handrails and handrail extensions such that 
the 300 mm encroachment into a right-of-way 
may not be achievable. 
3202.2.2  
Architectural features. Columns 
or pilasters, including bases and moldings, shall not 
project more than 300 mm. Belt courses, lintels, 
sills, 
architraves, 
pediments 
and 
similar 
architectural features shall not project more than 
100 mm.

❖ This 
section 
addresses 
the 
variety 
of 
architectural appurtenances that are part of a 
structure’s exterior wall or roof. Consistent with 
the projection limitations given for steps in 
Section 
3202.2.1 
, 
the 
maximum 
encroachment into the public way is 300 mm 
for columns and pilasters (see Commentary 
Figure 3202.2.1). Other noted architectural 
-

CHAPTER 32—ENCROACHMENTS INTO THE PUBLIC RIGHT-OF-WAY

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2024

features 
are 
limited 
to 
maximum 
encroachments of 100 mm. 
3202.2.3  
Awnings. The vertical clearance 
from the public right-of-way to the lowest part of 
any awning, including valances, shall be not less 
than 2.1 m.

❖ Awnings, including retractable awnings, are 
permitted to project into the public right-of-way. 
However, the clearance to any part of an 
awning, including its valance, is required to be 
a minimum of 2.1 m above grade (see 
Commentary Figure 3202.2.3). 
3202.3  Encroachments 2.4 m or more above 
grade. Encroachments 2.4 m or more above grade 
shall comply with Sections 3202.3.1 through 
3202.3.4 .

❖ This section prohibits all projections into the 
public right-of-way that are 2.4 m or more 
above grade, except those specifically allowed 
by Sections 3202.3.1 through 3202.3.4 . The 
maximum 
permitted 
encroachments 
and 
minimum clearances, as well as additional 
approval requirements, for the specified 
allowed components are detailed in their 
respective sections. 
3202.3.1  
Awnings, canopies, marquees 
and signs. Awnings, canopies, marquees and signs 
shall be constructed so as to support applicable 
loads as specified in Chapter 16. Awnings, 
canopies, marquees and signs with less than 4.5 m 
clearance above the sidewalk shall not extend into 
or occupy more than two-thirds the width of the 
sidewalk measured from the building. Stanchions or 
columns that support awnings, canopies, marquees 
and signs shall be located not less than 600 mm in 
from the curb line.
chunks.section_title: addresses encroachments on street lot lines

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3202_3_c20e7356
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ This 
section 
contains 
three 
distinct 
requirements. 
First, 
awnings, 
canopies, 
marquees and signs must be designed in 
accordance with the provisions of Chapter 16, 
with regard to the live loads that may be 
imposed on such structures. Second, the 
allowable projection of awnings, canopies, 
marquees and signs with less than 4.5 m of 
clearance above the sidewalk is limited to two-
thirds of the width of the sidewalk measured 
from the building. The standards of these 
sections apply to retractable awnings (defined 
in Section 202). Finally, the location of vertical 
structural components used for supporting 
awnings, canopies, marquees and signs 
(stanchions or columns) is also regulated. A 
minimum of 600 mm of clearance is required 
between the curb line and the vertical structural 
support for awnings, canopies, marquees and 
signs (see Commentary Figure 3202.3.1). 
3202.3.2  
Windows, 
balconies, 
architectural 
features 
and 
mechanical 
equipment. Where the vertical clearance above 
grade 
to 
projecting 
windows, 
balconies, 
architectural features or mechanical equipment is 
more than 2.4 m, 25 mm of encroachment is 
permitted for each additional 25 mm of clearance 
above 2.4 m, but the maximum encroachment shall 
be 1.2 m.

❖ Projections into the public right-of-way for 
appurtenances such as windows, balconies, 
architectural features or mechanical equipment 
more than 2.4 m above grade are allowed by 
this section. Such projections are limited to 25 
mm of encroachment for each additional 25 
mm of clearance above the minimum 2.4 m 
clearance required. However, the maximum 
allowable projection is limited to 1.2 m (see 
Commentary Figure 3202.3.2). 
3202.3.3  
Encroachments 4.5 m or more 
above grade. Encroachments 4.5 m or more above 
grade shall not be limited.

❖ Because encroachments into the public right-
of-way that are 4.5 m or more above grade do 
not interfere with or impede pedestrian or 
vehicular traffic, they are not regulated by the 
provisions of this chapter. 
3202.3.4  
Pedestrian 
walkways. 
The 
installation of a pedestrian walkway over a public 
right-of-way shall be subject to the approval of the 
applicable governing authority. The vertical 
clearance from the public right-of-way to the lowest 
part of a pedestrian walkway shall be not less than 
4.5 m.

❖ As in Section 3202.3.3 , projections of 
pedestrian walkways that have a minimum 
clearance of 4.5 m above grade do not interfere 
or impede pedestrian or vehicular traffic and 
thus are not prohibited by this section. The 
installation of pedestrian walkways over a 
public right-of-way is required to be approved 
by 
the 
governing 
authority. 
Detailed 
requirements for pedestrian walkways are 
provided in Section 3104. 
3202.4  Temporary 
encroachments. 
Where 
allowed by the applicable governing authority, 
vestibules and storm enclosures shall not be erected 
for a period of time exceeding 7 months in any 1 
year and shall not encroach more than 0.9 m nor 
more than one-fourth of the width of the sidewalk 
beyond the street lot line. Temporary entrance 
awnings shall be erected with a clearance of not less 
-

CHAPTER 32—ENCROACHMENTS INTO THE PUBLIC RIGHT-OF-WAY

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2025

than 2.1 m to the lowest portion of the hood or 
awning where supported on removable steel or 
other approved noncombustible support.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec3202_4_554af2df
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ This section addresses a special situation in 
which temporary projections across the street 
lot line are erected for climatic reasons (e.g., 
during the winter months some building owners 
may erect vestibule enclosures or canopies 
over entrances). The purpose of this section is 
to allow these energy-saving procedures, yet 
place 
limitations 
on 
such 
projections. 
Specifically, enclosures temporarily appended 
to an entranceway are permitted to project not 
more than 0.9 m beyond the street lot line, or 
up to one-fourth the width of the sidewalk. 
Whichever dimension results in the lesser 
projection defines the maximum projection. 
Additionally, when temporary awnings are 
erected, clearance between the sidewalk and 
the lowest part of the awning must be at least 
2.1 
m, 
which 
is 
consistent 
with 
the 
requirements for permanent 
awnings in 
Section 3202.2.3 . It is further required that the 
frame for the hood or awning is to be supported 
on removable steel 
or other approved 
noncombustible material. This last provision is 
necessitated by unforeseen changes in street 
lot lines, for example, that would result in the 
encroachment becoming an obstruction in the 
public right-of-way. 
-
