chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ See the commentary to Section N104.1. 
N104.1.2 
Structural plans, specifications 
and engineering details.  Where approval of the 
structural requirements of the replicable design is 
sought, the submittal documents shall include 
details for each wind region, seismic design 
category and climate zone for which approval is 
sought; and shall include the following: 
1 
Signed 
and 
sealed 
structural 
design 
calculations that support the member sizes on 
the drawings. 
2 
Design load criteria, including: live loads, 
wind loads, earthquake design date, and other 
special loads 
3 
Details of foundations and superstructure. 
4 
Provisions for special inspections.

❖ See the commentary to Section N104.1. 
N104.1.3 
Energy 
conservation 
details.  
Where approval of the energy conservation 
requirements of the replicable design is sought, the 
submittal documents shall include details for each 
climate zone for which approval is sought; and shall 
include the following: 
1 
Climate zones for which approval is sought. 
2 
Building envelope details. 
3 
Building mechanical system details. 
4 
Details of electrical power and lighting 
systems. 
-

APPENDIX N —REPLICABLE BUILDINGS

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2164

5 
Provisions for system commissioning.

❖ See the commentary to Section N104.1. 
SECTION N105 REVIEW AND 
APPROVAL OF REPLICABLE DESIGN 
N105.1 General. Proposed replicable designs shall 
be reviewed by an approved agency. The review 
shall be applicable only to the replicable design 
features submitted in accordance with Section 
N104. The review shall determine compliance with 
this code and additional codes specified in Section 
N104.1.

❖ This section and Section N106 and Section 
N107 lead to a building permit for replicable 
buildings. The replicable areas are all initially 
reviewed by an approved agency. An approved 
agency should be an independent, third-party 
agency that is competent in the technical area 
in question. 
N105.2 Documentation.  The results of the review 
shall be documented indicating compliance with the 
code requirements.

❖ Documentation of the review is necessary to 
ensure 
that 
all 
questions 
about 
code 
compliance have been addressed, and any 
deficiencies noted and corrected. 
N105.3 Deficiencies.  Where the review of the 
submitted 
construction 
documents 
identifies 
elements where the design is deficient and will not 
comply with the applicable code requirements, the 
approved agency shall notify the proponent of the 
replicable design, in writing, of the specific areas of 
noncompliance and request correction.

❖ A written notification is required in order to 
provide a documentation trail if there is any 
challenge later to code compliance. 
N105.4 Approval.  Where the review of the 
submitted construction documents determines that 
the design is in compliance with the codes 
designated in 
Section 
N104.1, and 
where 
deficiencies identified in Section N105.3 have been 
corrected the approved agency shall issue a 
summary report of Approved Replicable Design. 
The summary report shall include any limitations 
on the approved replicable design including, but not 
limited to climate zones, wind regions and seismic 
design categories.

❖ A summary report of compliance allows for the 
building official to review the replicable design 
and determine that a comprehensive review for 
code compliance has been completed. 
SECTION N106 SITE-SPECIFIC 
APPLICATION OF APPROVED 
REPLICABLE DESIGN 
N106.1 General. Where site-specific application of 
a replicable design that has been approved under the 
provisions of Section N105 is sought, the 
construction documents submitted to the building 
official shall comply with this section.

❖ Replicable design construction document 
requirements parallel the requirements for 
construction documents for any construction. 
The difference here is that the documents need 
to distinguish the portion that is part of the 
replicable design and other portions that are 
site specific. 
N106.2 Submittal 
documents. 
A 
summary 
description of the replicable design and related 
construction 
document 
shall 
be 
submitted. 
Construction documents shall be signed, sealed and 
dated by the registered design professional. A 
statement, signed, sealed and dated by the 
registered design professional, that the replicable 
design submitted for local review is the same as the 
replicable design reviewed by the approved agency, 
shall be submitted.

chunks.content:
❖ See the commentary to Section N106.1. 
N106.2.1 
Architectural 
plans

Architectural

1

❖ See the commentary to Section N106.1. 
SECTION N107 SITE-SPECIFIC REVIEW 
AND APPROVAL OF REPLICABLE 
DESIGN 
N107.1 General. 
Proposed 
site-specific 
application of replicable design shall be submitted 
to the building official in accordance with the 
provisions of Chapter 1 and Appendix N.

❖ As with any building, the approval must be 
based on compliance with code for the specific 
site at which the building is located. 
-

APPENDIX N —REPLICABLE BUILDINGS

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2165

N107.2 Site-specific review and approval of 
replicable design.  The building official shall 
verify that the replicable design submitted for site-
specific application is the same as the approved 
replicable design reviewed by the approved agency. 
In addition, the building official shall review the 
following for code compliance. 
1 
Construction documents for variations from 
the replicable design. 
2 
Construction for portions of the building that 
are not part of the replicable design. 
3 
Documents for local requirements as 
identified by the building official.

❖ See the commentary to Section N107.1. 
-

APPENDIX O PERFORMANCE-BASED APPLICATION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2166

APPENDIX O PERFORMANCE-BASED APPLICATION

The provisions contained in this appendix are 
not mandatory unless specifically referenced in 
the adopting ordinance.

General Comments

The provisions contained in this appendix are 
not mandatory unless specifically referenced in 
the adopting ordinance, as stated in Section 
101.2.1.

The regulations contained in this appendix 
address the growing use of performance-based 
design approaches and the need to provide a 
stronger framework for the approvals process. 
The ICCPC contains many of these concepts 
within its administrative Chapter 1 ; however, 
many designs that are designed through a 
performance-based or alternative process are 
working directly through the building code. This 
appendix 
addresses 
the 
key 
process 
components that should be addressed during 
such design reviews and approvals including 
qualifications, proper construction document 
preparation, the review process, construction 
phase and the long-term use of such buildings. 
This includes what should be included in the 
design documentation and how compliance is 
verified.

❖ Purpose

The purpose of this appendix is to provide a 
resource and possibly an adopted appendix 
that can provide a stronger framework for the 
review and approval of performance-based 
designs, 
commonly 
termed 
“alternative 
designs,” beyond simple demonstration of 
equivalency. This includes what documentation 
should be provided and how compliance is 
properly verified. Concepts such as the use of 
peer review are introduced as a tool in this 
process. 
SECTION O101 GENERAL 
O101.1 Introduction. 
The 
following 
administrative provisions are excerpted from the 
ICC Performance Code for Buildings and Facilities 
and can be used in conjunction with the Alternate 
Methods provisions in Chapter 1, or for a review of 
submittals requiring a rational analysis or 
performance-based 
design. 
These 
provisions 
provide an established framework for the building 
official in terms of the design expertise needed, the 
necessary submittals, a review framework and 
related items.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec1613_20_874debb5
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ This appendix is intended to provide key 
aspects of the International Code Council 
Performance Code for Buildings and Facilities 
(ICCPC) from an administrative perspective. 
The ICCPC is often not adopted but provides 
some valuable tools that could be of assistance 
to building and fire officials when presented 
with performance-based designs. It should be 
clarified that most performance-based designs 
are akin to alternative designs and often are not 
fully performance based. In other words, it is 
usually only a small aspect of a building that is 
designed in such a way that it deviates from the 
prescriptive SBC 201. As this section states, 
this appendix is focused on qualifications, 
construction document needs and concepts for 
proper review of such designs. This appendix 
could also supplement the information found in 
Section 104.11. 
O101.2 Qualifications. 
Registered 
design 
professionals shall possess the knowledge, skills 
and abilities necessary to demonstrate compliance 
with this code.

❖ The 
qualifications 
of 
registered 
design 
professionals their knowledge of design, 
analysis, 
research, 
computation, 
documentation and professional standards in 
their respective areas of expertise—are 
prerequisites to a successful application of 
performance-based 
design 
provisions. 
Qualifications, to a certain extent, are regulated 
by professional registration laws within the 
state or jurisdiction in which the project is to be 
constructed.

In addition to the registration requirements that 
may exist within the jurisdiction, the building 
official may require a formal declaration of 
education, training and experience of all design 
team members to ensure that sufficient 
expertise has been retained in all technical 
subject areas of the performance-based 
design. The performance-based solution is a 
systems approach, and all components must be 
addressed with a proper level of sophistication 
that can span many design disciplines.

Registered design professionals and other 
members of the design team (such as 
architects; engineers; interior, theater and 
kitchen designers; fire modelers; and computer 
-

APPENDIX O PERFORMANCE-BASED APPLICATION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2167

support staff) have a responsibility to gain 
technical skills and performance-based design 
skills 
through 
professional 
or 
university 
programs before embarking on a design project 
using performance-based design techniques. 
The intent is that the design team must consist 
of sufficiently qualified individuals to carry out 
the responsibilities necessary to design the 
proposed project.

The minimum qualifications and skills of the 
design professional will vary significantly based 
on the type of project and the degree of analysis 
that the project requires. Performance-based 
designs require more analysis than the same 
design undertaken using a prescriptive code or 
standard. The main difference is that the 
decision-making process involves multiple 
objectives to demonstrate compliance with 
performance 
provisions 
and 
acceptable 
methods. It follows, then, that increased project 
documentation of these many decisions and 
corresponding solutions will be necessary. 
Therefore, the qualifications and skill levels for 
design professionals may vary as a function of 
the size and complexity of the project design. It 
is incumbent upon the design professional to 
become competent in any areas of practice 
where 
new 
skills 
are 
required 
before 
undertaking a project based on performance 
design techniques.

The increased qualifications and required skills 
are particularly important for those designers 
that utilize modeling techniques. Design-team 
leadership skills also need increased attention 
and enhancement to ensure all aspects of the 
design are coordinated and meet the intent of 
the 
code. 
This 
will 
require 
extensive 
coordination skills in the leader to enable team 
members to produce a system design with 
compatible components. As noted previously, 
many projects will be only partly based on 
performance design; many of the design 
features will remain prescriptive and must 
interface 
with 
the 
performance-design 
components.

Large, complex projects using performance 
approaches would most likely require highly 
qualified and skilled engineers in the structural, 
electrical, fire and mechanical disciplines that 
are necessary to undertake a major, complex 
building design. 
O101.3 Construction document preparation. 
Construction documents required by this code shall 
be prepared in adequate detail and submitted for 
review and approval in accordance with Section 
107.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec1613_21_39049592
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ Whether a project is based on the prescriptive 
building code or is a performance-based 
design, proper construction documentation is 
necessary. There is no reason to treat 
performance designs differently in that respect 
as ultimately the design needs to be translated 
into documents that will describe clearly how a 
building is to be constructed. 
O101.3.1 
Review. Construction documents 
submitted in accordance with this code shall be 
reviewed for code compliance with the appropriate 
code provisions in accordance with Section 107.

❖ The review process is necessary to verify that 
the 
construction 
documents 
meet 
the 
requirements of the code. Most performance-
based designs depend primarily on the 
prescriptive code for much of the building 
design, as only certain aspects of the building 
design are performance-based. Therefore, 
much of the review will be based on the 
building code. It is important that construction 
documents are clear as to where each aspect 
of the building design originates such that it is 
clear whether it is linked to a prescriptive code 
or 
performance-based 
solution. 
The 
prescriptive building code may require a 
different fire-resistance rating, for example, 
than the performance-based design requires. 
These differences need to be understood 
during the construction document review. 
O101.4 Construction. Construction shall comply 
with 
the 
approved 
construction 
documents 
submitted in accordance with this code, and shall be 
verified and approved to demonstrate compliance 
with this code.

❖ Regardless of what the design was based on, 
construction must be based on the approved 
construction documents. Any variations must 
be accounted for and verified to ensure that the 
change does not affect the performance of the 
building adversely. This may require a special 
review if it affects aspects of the building 
designed using performance-based methods. 
O101.4.1 
Facility operating policies and 
procedures. Policies, operations, training and 
procedures shall comply with approved documents 
submitted in accordance with this code, and shall be 
verified and approved to demonstrate compliance 
with this code.

❖ Some performance-based designs come with 
unique requirements that must be maintained, 
such as restrictions on occupant load or 
contents that may otherwise would not be 
regulated by the prescriptive building code. 
However, designs should be robust enough to 
still 
perform 
as 
intended 
with 
minor 
-

APPENDIX O PERFORMANCE-BASED APPLICATION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2168

modifications. These documents are key to the 
long-term successful life of a building and must 
be reviewed and approved. 
O101.4.2 
Maintenance. Maintenance of the 
performance-based design 
shall be 
ensured 
throughout the life of the building or portion 
thereof.

❖ As noted in Section O101.4.2, where there are 
unique characteristics of a building that need to 
be maintained to continue to function as 
intended, those 
characteristics 
must 
be 
properly maintained. One could argue this is 
true of many aspects of the prescriptive code 
requirements, such as fire barriers or automatic 
sprinkler systems. Maintenance is key in all 
buildings regardless of how they are designed. 
Most systems and features of the building will 
still be typical features and systems, but they 
may be used in slightly different applications 
and need to be maintained as approved. For 
example, the fire protection system may have 
a different testing schedule than typically 
prescribed.

Once a building or facility is constructed and 
approved, the owner is responsible for 
maintaining it in accordance with the approved 
operations and maintenance manual. Critical 
areas determined by the design professional 
may require inspection, testing or service at 
predetermined intervals to ensure continued 
compliance for the life of the building. Like other 
areas of noncompliance, the performance-
based design aspect of the building must be 
maintained.

The maintenance responsibilities of an owner of 
a performance-based design facility can require 
a higher level of accountability than if the facility 
were built only to prescriptive construction 
codes. It is intended that the building officials 
who enforce the building, residential, electrical, 
fire, mechanical and plumbing codes be part of 
the review process for buildings and facilities 
approved under this code and for enforcement 
of future maintenance provisions. The building 
officials who enforce maintenance and fire code 
provisions must be adequately prepared for 
inspection 
of 
performance-based 
design 
buildings and facilities in accordance with the 
operations and maintenance manual.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec1613_22_c0c8f933
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
This section of the appendix does not mandate 
a particular approach, but options include a 
conditional certificate of occupancy or, where 
the features are extremely critical, a deed 
restriction to maintain an active certificate of 
occupancy. Failure to comply with these 
conditions or a deed restriction could result in 
revocation or nonrenewal of the certificate of 
occupancy. 
O101.4.3 
Changes. The owner or the 
owner's authorized agent shall be responsible to 
ensure that any change to the facility, process, or 
system does not increase the hazard level beyond 
that originally designed without approval and that 
changes shall be documented in accordance with 
the code.

❖ It is the responsibility of the owner to properly 
address any changes that could potentially 
exceed the conditions approved in the original 
design. Where a building, structure or facility is 
proposed to be remodeled, to be renovated or 
to undergo a significant change of use, a 
qualified design professional should evaluate 
the existing construction documents for the 
proposed change and submit a written report of 
the findings and recommendations. The 
building official then should evaluate the written 
report, intended design, or change of use to 
determine if the proposal adversely impacts the 
existing building or facility and to determine 
compliance with this code based on prior 
approved performance design documents. 
Based on the findings, written comments 
should be provided to the design professional 
indicating either approval of the change or the 
requirements for correcting any adverse 
findings. Once the report is approved in 
concept, the design professional can proceed 
with the design or initiate the change of use, 
which may require a new or updated certificate 
of occupancy. 
O101.5 Documentation. The registered design 
professional 
shall 
prepare 
appropriate 
documentation for the project, clearly detailing the 
approach and rationale for the design submittal, the 
construction and the future use of the building, 
facility or process.

❖ This section is intended to clarify the full scope 
of documentation required for a project, which 
might vary significantly based on the building 
official's discretion and the complexity of the 
project. Performance-based designs require a 
much higher level of documentation since they 
do not simply follow prescriptive requirements. 
However, often much of the overall design is 
still based on the prescriptive building code.

Complete documentation that provides a clear 
record of objectives and decisions made to 
provide a safe building, including associated 
use and maintenance responsibilities, can be a 
substantial benefit to the building official's staff 
and the public after completion of a project. 
-

APPENDIX O PERFORMANCE-BASED APPLICATION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2169

This documentation must also contain any 
conditions unique to this building that would 
require 
special 
maintenance 
or 
certain 
procedures that may not be anticipated with a 
typical building based on a purely prescriptive 
approach. This can be done by the conditional 
certificate of occupancy that highlights certain 
“bounding conditions” to provide public notice to 
future buyers, lenders or any other persons who 
may be reviewing legal conditions via a due 
diligence review. 
O101.5.1 
Reports and manuals. The design 
report shall document the steps taken in the design 
analysis, clearly identifying the criteria, parameters, 
inputs, assumptions, sensitivities and limitations 
involved in the analysis. The design report shall 
clearly identify bounding conditions, assumptions 
and sensitivities that clarify the expected uses and 
limitations of the performance analysis. This report 
shall verify that the design approach is in 
compliance with the applicable codes and 
acceptable methods and shall be submitted for 
concurrence by the building official prior to the 
construction documents being completed. The 
report shall document the design features to be 
incorporated based on the analysis. 
The design report shall address the following: 
1 
Project scope. 
2 
Goals and objectives. 
3 
Performance criteria. 
4 
Hazard scenarios. 
5 
Design fire loads and hazards. 
6 
Final design. 
7 
Evaluation. 
8 
Bounding conditions and critical design 
assumptions. 
9 
Critical design features. 
10 System design and operational requirements. 
11 Operational and maintenance requirements. 
12 Commissioning testing requirements and 
acceptance criteria. 
13 Frequency of certificate renewal. 
14 Supporting documents and references. 
15 Preliminary site and floor plans.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec1613_23_ed880c1d
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ The design report documents: project scope, 
goals and objectives; steps taken for analytical 
analyses with identification of performance 
criteria, parameter input assumptions and 
limitations; 
magnitude 
of 
design 
loads; 
computer model data and scenarios for 
evaluating acceptance ranges; final design; 
evaluation; criteria design assumptions with 
bounding conditions, critical design features 
and 
final 
design 
bounding 
conditions; 
commissioning 
testing 
requirements 
with 
acceptance criteria; and supporting documents 
and 
references. 
The 
registered 
design 
professional in responsible charge should 
provide copies of supporting documents and 
references necessary for a professional review 
to 
be 
completed. 
Design 
guidelines, 
handbooks and other applicable authoritative 
documents 
prepared 
by 
professional 
associations may expand or further clarify 
applications of the items listed. 
O101.5.2 
Design 
submittal. 
Applicable 
construction documents shall be submitted to the 
building official for review. The documents shall be 
submitted in accordance with the jurisdiction's 
procedures and in sufficient detail to obtain 
appropriate permits.

❖ The design professional(s) must coordinate all 
design documentation for compatibility and 
completeness to be submitted to the building 
official. 
Design 
documents 
must 
clearly 
indicate the areas where performance and 
prescriptive codes apply, so that it is clear to 
the reviewer which prescriptive code provisions 
are applicable.

Documents should be submitted in accordance 
with the procedures of the jurisdiction and in 
sufficient detail to obtain permits for the project. 
Reports and preliminary documents required by 
the building official, such as concept reports, 
design 
reports 
and 
operations 
and 
maintenance manuals, may be required earlier 
than full design document submittal to obtain 
permits so that conceptual or phase reviews 
can be made. 
O101.6 Review. 
Construction 
documents 
submitted in accordance with this code shall be 
reviewed for code compliance with the appropriate 
code provisions.

❖ Before approving and issuing a permit, the 
building official or other designated individuals 
are responsible to review or ensure that 
construction documents meet the requirements 
of the performance code as well as the 
prescriptive code provisions that are being 
utilized as part of the design. Although 
performance-based designs will be submitted a 
small percentage of the time, they will likely 
complicate the plan review process. This 
depends on the scope of the design that is 
based 
on 
performance-based 
design 
-

APPENDIX O PERFORMANCE-BASED APPLICATION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2170

methodologies. There will be many designs 
that are primarily based on the prescriptive 
codes but incorporate minor performance-
based elements.

Performance-based design typically cannot 
depend solely upon designs being signed off on 
by a registered design professional without 
additional review, Registration and a license to 
practice 
engineering 
do 
not 
necessarily 
constitute 
acceptable 
qualifications 
to 
undertake a performance-based design or 
remove the need for review by the building 
official or a third-party reviewer. 
• 
The building official can still undertake review 
in a traditional manner for most projects. 
Where performance-based elements are 
incorporated it is particularly important that 
the building official evaluate the skills of the 
department before undertaking such a 
review. Understanding review limitations and 
addressing these limitations ahead of time 
will help to ensure a successful and thorough 
review. This understanding of limitations 
includes 
evaluating 
the 
educational 
qualifications and experience needed to 
review performance-based designs and 
analyses. The building official may consider 
one or more of the following options to 
develop a plan for ensuring qualified plan 
review: 
• 
Determine 
if 
the 
staff 
has 
sufficient 
education, 
engineering 
or 
architectural 
registrations, 
and 
levels 
of 
continuing 
education needed to perform the tasks. 
• 
Initiate a program to upgrade plan reviewers’ 
educational and professional qualifications. 
• 
Acquire contract review services via a 
consultant plan reviewer with applicable 
qualifications. 
• 
Utilize a peer review process that will provide 
individuals or a team meeting an appropriate 
level of qualifications, standards of ethics and 
accountability. Guidelines for Peer Review in 
the Fire Protection Design Process is a 
resource for undertaking peer review that can 
assist with this process.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec1613_24_2f9ce154
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ When the building official accepts a consultant 
as a contract reviewer in lieu of their own staff's 
review 
of 
construction 
documents, 
documentation must be provided for the 
building official's acceptance indicating that the 
performance and prescriptive code provisions 
have been met. This document should become 
part of the official records for the project and 
the basis of approving the design documents 
and issuing permits.

After 
the 
design 
documents, 
supporting 
documents and reports are verified as meeting 
this code and other applicable codes, permits 
should be issued in accordance with building 
official procedures. 
O101.6.1 
Peer review. The owner or the 
owner's authorized agent shall be responsible for 
retaining and furnishing the services of a registered 
design professional or recognized expert, who will 
perform as a peer reviewer, where required and 
approved by the building official.

❖ This section is simply denoting that if a peer 
review is necessary it is the responsibility of the 
owner to coordinate and pay for such process. 
The peer reviewer must be approved by the 
building official. It is possible in some 
jurisdictions they may have a preferred list of 
approved peer reviewers to simplify and 
facilitate this process. 
O101.6.2 
Costs. The costs of special 
services, including contract review, where required 
by the building official, shall be borne by the owner 
or the owner's authorized agent.

❖ Costs for review of design documents and for 
inspections should be the burden of the owner. 
Since costs for services are expected to 
exceed the fees collected for typical plan 
review and inspection services, the building 
official should amend the code to include cost 
recovery for services rendered on all projects 
using performance approaches. 
O101.7 Permits. Prior to the start of construction, 
appropriate permits shall be obtained in accordance 
with the jurisdiction's procedures and applicable 
codes.

❖ This section ensures that, regardless of the 
type of project, all proper permits are obtained 
before construction begins. 
O101.8 Verification 
of 
compliance. 
Upon 
completion of the project, documentation shall be 
prepared that verifies performance and prescriptive 
code provisions have been met. Where required by 
the building official, the registered design 
professional shall file a report that verifies 
bounding conditions are met.

❖ Verifying that a building as constructed 
complies with the design that was approved is 
critical. This verification comes in the form of 
proper documentation.

This section is intended to clarify the full scope 
of documentation required for a project, which 
might vary significantly based on the building 
official's discretion and the complexity of the 
-

APPENDIX O PERFORMANCE-BASED APPLICATION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2171

project. Performance-based designs require a 
much higher level of documentation. Complete 
documentation that provides a clear record of 
objectives and decisions made to provide a 
safe building, including associated use and 
maintenance 
responsibilities, 
can 
be 
a 
substantial benefit to the building official 's staff 
and the public after completion of a project. In 
some cases, performance-based designs may 
have specific “bounding conditions,” such as a 
limit on types of events or combustible material, 
that will require documentation both when the 
building is first occupied and for the life of the 
building. 
O101.9 Extent of documentation. Approved 
construction documents, the operations and 
maintenance manual, inspection and testing 
records, and certificates of occupancy with 
conditions shall be included in the project 
documentation of the building official's records.

❖ Documents from the initial approved design 
through operation and maintenance manuals 
must be included in the official record to foster 
ongoing 
compliance. 
Without 
this 
documentation, the possibilities that the unique 
aspects of the design would be lost and the 
building not properly operated or maintained 
increase. The documentation required would 
need to include any “bounding conditions.” 
O101.10 
Analysis of change. The registered 
design professional shall evaluate the existing 
building, facilities, premises, processes, and 
contents, and the applicable documentation of the 
proposed change as it affects portions of the 
building, facility, premises, processes and contents 
that were previously designed for compliance under 
a performance-based code. Prior to any change that 
was not documented in a previously approved 
design, the registered design professional shall 
examine 
the 
applicable 
design 
documents, 
bounding conditions, operation and maintenance 
manuals, and deed restrictions.

chunks.chunk_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200_sec1613_25_c3e828bb
chunks.code_id: SBC 201 - The Saudi General Building Code-2001-2200
chunks.content:
❖ As discussed in Section O101.4.3, it is critical 
that a registered design professional address 
any changes to a building that was designed 
and constructed based on a performance-
based design. See the commentary to Section 
O101.4.3. 
-

APPENDIX O PERFORMANCE-BASED APPLICATION

SBC 201-CC-2024 
2172

Bibliography 
The following resource material was used in the preparation of the commentary for this appendix of 
the code. 
Guidelines for Peer Review in the Fire Protection Design Process. Gaithersburg, MD: Society of Fire 
Protection Engineers (SFPE), 2020 
-
